interactions.summary: Jiang turns to Greece, naming Homer, alphabet, and polis as the innovation base; contrasts Spartan land slavery and oligarchy with Athenian maritime expansion and democracy; then shows Athens, Sparta, and Thebes reaching equilibrium while Macedonia learns from all three.

He maps Alexander’s conquest of Persia onto the Qin pattern: elite overproduction and disloyal elites open the empire from inside, while Darius thinks he can bribe Alexander because equilibrium makes him underestimate unlimited conqueror appetite.

claims.claim: Jiang credits Greek innovation to Homer, alphabetic writing, and the polis.
He explains Sparta as a conservative oligarchic land society built around controlling Helot labor, while Athens becomes a democratic naval society because rowers fight and therefore vote.
Macedonia arises when Greek city-states reach equilibrium; Philip II combines the military innovations of Sparta, Thebes, and Athens and recruits their talent.
Alexander’s Persian conquest is explained through elite disloyalty and equilibrium blindness, not merely battlefield superiority.

signature_moments.moment: Greek civilization is made from Homer, the alphabet, and the polis: myth, transmission, and citizen politics in one package.
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This gives Greece its creative engine before the lecture turns it into empire.

signature_moments.moment: A navy forces democracy because anyone can row.
signature_moments.source_phrase: if you're a navy, you have to be a democracy
signature_moments.why_it_matters: The political form is derived from military technology and participation, not abstract ideals.

signature_moments.moment: Darius offers territory and treasury, but Alexander wants all of it because he thinks he is the son of God.
signature_moments.why_it_matters: The equilibrium bargain fails against an unlimited sacred conqueror.

glossary_terms.definition_or_usage: Greek citizen political unit where citizens participate in public life; often small but numerous.
Conquered slave/serf population whose labor supports Sparta and whose rebellion risk keeps Sparta conservative.
Poor, backward, formerly marginal power that unites Greece by absorbing Greek military and talent innovations.

chronology_notes.note: Jiang flags Rome as the next power after the Hellenistic equilibrium; this lecture stops before the Rome class.
